utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: miss edwards is here for evaluation of facial pain this is a 54 -year-old male

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: how're you doing doctor cruz nice to see you today

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: good to see you mister edwards i'm doctor

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: tell me what's been going on

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah so i've got this stabbing shooting pain i've had for a while you know and it it especially right here in my right face right in that cheek bone area it's sometimes it gets super hot i've had it for i think a couple of months now and i went to my family doctor and they said i should come see you

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay great i would like to ask you a little bit more about that

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: had in your face do you remember how long that you had it

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: probably for about two or three months just just came on slowly i started noticing it but now it's i have episodes where just shooting stabbing kind of a pain in my right cheek bone right face area

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: mm-hmm do you have any sensory loss meaning like numbness or tingling in that part of your face

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: not that i've noticed no

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay do you have any symptoms like this anywhere else on your face including the other side of your face

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no it's just on the right side mostly not on the left side just on the right side of my face

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: have you noticed any weakness on that side of the face like when you smile or while you're doing other things any weakness there

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: not really i mean i've i've i've tried to you know when i smile my smile seems equal on both sides my eyes i do n't have any weakness there my vision seems to be unchanged but just this stabbing severe pain it's just like excruciating pain that i get sometimes does it happen on its own or there are certain things that trigger it

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sometimes if i'm if certain temperatures seem to trigger it sometimes or if it's super where it's cold i get a trigger sometimes

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sometimes certain kind of sensory outside of the wind sometimes that seems to trigger it but that's about it

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and anything that you've done to to that helps for a little bit when you

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: pain excess

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: you know i've tried ibuprofen and motrin that had really has n't helped it just comes on suddenly and then it's kinda stabbing excruciating pain i've tried rubbing some you know some tiger balm on it that did n't work

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: but that's you know so i i went to my family doctor and he said you know i really need to see a neurosurgeon

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: got it how long do these episodes last these pain

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: you know it can last for anywhere from a few minutes to sometimes about an hour but generally generally a few minutes

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: any history of something like multiple sclerosis or any brain tumors that you know

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no nothing like that alright any other kinda headache symptoms that have anything like you had migraines or anything related to the headaches i mean i get occasional headaches but not really i do n't have a history of migraines but i occasionally get headaches like everybody else i take some tylenol that usually goes away but this is different

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: how severe is the pain on a scale of one to ten

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: when it comes on it's like a ten it's like somebody is stabbing you with an ice pick and but usually you know after a few minutes usually it goes away but sometimes it can last up to an hour

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: great so mister i would like to do a physical exam if that's okay with you

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sure

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i would like you to follow my finger here and i see that you're following my finger in in both directions can you show me your teeth

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: that looks nice and symmetric i'm gon na rub my fingers next to your ear can you hear that

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yep

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: you can hear it on the other side as well

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yep

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i'm gon na take this cotton tape and run it along the side of your face can you feel that okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah it's a little bit numb on my right side not so much on my left side

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright i'm gon na use this little needle here and i'm gon na poke here and i wan na see if you feel like it's being sharp or dull on that part of your face does that feel different or normal

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it feels a little bit dull on my left on my right side my left side it feels sharp

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright good well i had a chance to look at your mri

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and i looked at your mri and it appears to have small blood vessel that is abutting and perhaps even pinching the trigeminal nerve the trigeminal nerve is nerve that comes from the brainstem that goes out to the face and provides the sensory inflammation from the face and you may have a condition called trigeminal neuralgia

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: where the nerve compression causes this kind of shooting electrical pain in the face

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: how do we treat it

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: well the first line would be to try some medications usually we start with medications that are called gabapentin

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: or tegretol these are medications that really help reduce the excitability of the nerve

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: most people can get the pain control with that but there are some people where the medications are n't gon na be enough and in that situation we would consider surgery i would n't i would n't recommend that now we usually try the medications first

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: for considering a surgery to decompress the nerve the root cause of the problem is the compression of the blood vessel against the nerve

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so we should be tried which one would you recommend the tegretol or yeah i think we could start with the tegretol to start with i just want to make sure that you understand some of the potential side effects that you can have with this

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: sure

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: it's always a little bit of trial and error to figure out what the right dosing that would work for you but some common side effects can include you know memory loss tingling imbalance some people can actually have like dermatologic

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: skin reaction to this medication and particularly people who have eustachian descent so that we may do some genetic testing just to make sure that it will be safe for you

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay sounds good let's do it

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so i will prescribe that for you and then we will see how that goes and if your pain continues we can talk about different surgical options to treat the pain

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i think i would like to try the tegretol first and if that does n't work then i can come back and so once should i come back and just to kinda check back with you and see if you know if it's had enough time for a fact or not

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: i think one month would be a great time to follow up

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay sounds good

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so if you want to send that prescription over to my pharmacy that would be fine and then why do n't i come back in about a month and we'll go from there

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: great i'll see you then

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright

detail_list.summary: The patient is a 54-year-old male presenting with right-sided facial pain.
Facial pain
The patient reports stabbing, shooting pain in the right cheekbone area for approximately two months.
The doctor asks about the duration of the facial pain.
The patient reports the pain has lasted two to three months and occurs in episodes of shooting, stabbing pain in the right cheek.
The doctor asks if the patient has sensory loss, numbness, or tingling in the face.
The patient denies noticing any numbness or tingling.
The doctor asks if the patient has similar symptoms on the other side of the face.
The patient denies left-sided facial pain, confirming it is only on the right side.
The doctor asks if the patient has noticed any facial weakness.
The patient denies facial weakness, vision changes, or eye weakness.
The patient reports that cold temperatures can trigger the pain.
The patient reports that wind can trigger the pain.
The patient reports that ibuprofen, Motrin, and tiger balm have not helped the pain.
The doctor asks about the duration of the pain episodes.
The patient reports pain episodes typically last a few minutes, but can last up to an hour.
The doctor asks about a history of multiple sclerosis or brain tumors.
The patient denies a history of multiple sclerosis, brain tumors, or migraines, though he reports occasional headaches.
The doctor asks the patient to rate the severity of the pain on a scale of one to ten.
The patient rates the pain as a ten out of ten.
The doctor declares the intent to perform a physical exam.
The doctor observes symmetric teeth and normal eye movement.
The doctor performs a finger rub test.
The patient confirms hearing the finger rub.
The doctor performs a light touch test on the face.
The patient reports numbness on the right side of the face compared to the left.
The doctor performs a pinprick test on the face.
The patient reports the pinprick feels dull on the right side and sharp on the left side.
The doctor reviews the MRI results.
The MRI shows a blood vessel pinching the trigeminal nerve, and the doctor diagnoses trigeminal neuralgia.
The patient asks about treatment options.
The doctor recommends starting with gabapentin or Tegretol.
The doctor explains that surgery is an option if medication is ineffective, but it is not recommended at this time.
The doctor recommends starting with Tegretol.
The doctor discusses potential side effects of Tegretol, including memory loss, tingling, imbalance, and dermatologic reactions.
The doctor mentions the need for genetic testing for patients of East Asian descent due to the risk of skin reactions.
The doctor will prescribe Tegretol and discuss surgical options if pain persists.
The doctor schedules a follow-up appointment in one month.
